notes: Deep dive on Virginia datacenter opposition. VA currently has 57 entries in fights.json. This research found 8 genuinely new entries not yet tracked plus 12 status updates for existing entries. Covers: budget special session (April 23), new GA bills passed, Amazon/GW campus fight in Loudoun, Fairfax County land sale, Gigaland resubmission, PWC overlay amendment, New Kent TOD, and more.

new_entries.summary: Amazon Data Services purchased George Washington University's 122-acre Science and Technology Campus in Ashburn for $427 million with plans to convert it into a data center campus. Loudoun County government was blindsided by the sale and is vowing to fight the conversion. County economic development director Buddy Rizer stated data centers were never envisioned for this property and the county has spent 20 years keeping data centers off Route 7. Under new Loudoun zoning rules, data centers cannot be built by-right and require special exception review before the Board of Supervisors, which is expected to oppose the proposal. Amazon retains GW usage rights for up to 5 years.

new_entries.company: Starwood Capital Group (SCG Global Holdings LLC)
new_entries.summary: Fairfax County Board of Supervisors held a public hearing on March 17, 2026 on a proposed $166.8 million sale of 41.7 acres of county-owned property at 3721 Stonecroft Blvd in Chantilly to Starwood Capital Group for data center development. The site currently houses police training facilities including a firearms range and driver training area, located south of Dulles Airport. Environmental groups protest insufficient transparency and the impact of diesel generators. Bren Pointe HOA proposed a land-swap alternative. Proceeds would fund a new police training campus. Expected to generate $20M+ annual tax revenue.

New Kent County is developing a Technology Overlay District spanning 1,800 acres along the Route 33 corridor, following Goochland County's lead. County consultants identified 11 ideal and 42 high-potential data center sites. A controversial fast-track application process would allow approval without public hearings, sparking opposition from residents concerned about water impacts and loss of farmland. Public meetings scheduled for late March and early April 2026 ahead of Planning Commission and Board of Supervisors hearings in spring/summer. County faces millions in costs to safeguard future water supplies.

new_entries.project_name: HB 507 - Data Center Generator Emission Standards
new_entries.summary: HB 507 by Del. John McAuliff (D-Fauquier) passed both chambers of the General Assembly and awaits Gov. Spanberger's signature. The bill mandates DEQ deny any air permit for a data center generator submitted after July 2026 unless equipped with Tier IV engines or environmental equivalent. A direct response to VCU research showing Northern Virginia data center diesel generators emit more air pollutants than natural gas power plants. Would be the first state law mandating specific emission standards for data center backup generators.

SB 94 (Sen. Danica Roem) and HB 153 (Del. Josh Thomas) require developers of facilities using 100+ megawatts to submit site assessment reports examining impacts on agriculture, parks, historic sites, forestland, and noise within 500 feet. The Senate version includes a requirement to restrict data centers to industrial zones; the House version does not. Conference committee deadlocked. A similar bill passed in 2025 but was vetoed by then-Gov. Youngkin.

SB 553 (Sen. Kannan Srinivasan, D-Loudoun) and HB 496 (Del. Liz Guzman, D-Prince William) require public disclosure of data center water consumption. The Senate version directs water utilities to report aggregate water use to the State Water Control Board; the House version requires consumption estimates for local rezoning/permit requests. In conference committee with six legislators. Would be Virginia's first data center water transparency requirement.

new_entries.project_name: Special Session - Data Center Sales Tax Exemption
new_entries.summary: Gov. Spanberger called a special session for April 23, 2026 after the General Assembly failed to pass a budget due to a stalemate over the $1.6 billion/year data center sales tax exemption. Senate Appropriations Chair Louise Lucas insists on ending the exemption by 2027 (8 years before the 2035 sunset). House Appropriations Chair Luke Torian wants to keep it, arguing Virginia cannot renege on existing contracts. Spanberger has signaled caution about eliminating the exemption. The exemption covers sales tax on computer equipment for data centers investing $150M+ and creating 50+ jobs. Bloomberg Tax called it 'Virginia's largest single tax expenditure.'

Loudoun County launched Phase 2 of its multi-year data center regulatory overhaul. Phase 1 (March 2025) eliminated by-right data center development; Phase 2 focuses on Comprehensive Plan changes, conditional use evaluation guidance, utility substation definitions, onsite power generation/energy storage rules, building height standards, noise regulations, and parking requirements. Draft text amendments presented to the board in May 2026, Planning Commission public comment in July, with potential final board adoption in December 2026. Represents the most comprehensive data center regulatory framework in any US county.

updates.summary: Gigaland LLC resubmitted a scaled-down version of their data center proposal in January 2026, reducing building square footage by 55% (from 2.22M to 998,662 sq ft), cutting from 7 to 6 buildings at 45 ft height (down from 80 ft), and halving power demand from 1.2 GW to 600 MW (two 300 MW substations instead of four). Estimated investment: $3.067 billion, 100+ permanent jobs. Fauquier County supervisors remain skeptical - Marshall District Supervisor Regan Washer said he has 'no interest in their proposed resubmission.' Scott District Supervisor Ike Broaddus called it 'a heavy lift.' Application is under review by the Planning Commission.

Goochland County supervisors approved $250K in February 2026 to hire outside counsel to defend a lawsuit filed December 4, 2025 by four property owners (Haas, Knisley, Reed, Minnick) challenging the November 2025 approval of the Technology Overlay District. The lawsuit alleges the TOD ordinance contains provisions 'without adequately objective standards,' includes internal inconsistencies, and delegates overly broad discretion to county officials. Separately, New Kent County is now following Goochland's lead in creating its own TOD. The lawsuit is proceeding in Goochland Circuit Court.

The SCC dismissed SELC's petition for reconsideration in early 2026 and reaffirmed its approval of Dominion's $1.47B, 944 MW Chesterfield Energy Reliability Center. SELC then pivoted to a new legal front: on behalf of Chesterfield NAACP, CASA Inc., Appalachian Voices, and Mothers Out Front, they appealed DEQ's air permit in Richmond Circuit Court, alleging violations of the Virginia Environmental Justice Act, Clean Economy Act, State Air Pollution Control Law, and federal Clean Air Act. The appeal argues DEQ failed to analyze disproportionate health impacts on nearby low-income communities and communities of color. Construction anticipated to begin 2026, completion 2029.

Valley Link (a joint venture of Dominion Energy, Transource, and FirstEnergy Transmission) plans to file with the Virginia SCC for route approval by summer 2026 (approximately September). The proposed 115-mile, 765 kV transmission line from Campbell County to Culpeper would clear thousands of acres and impact hundreds of property owners. Community meetings and open houses are underway to gather input on potential routes. SCC decision anticipated around September 2027. Opposition groups including Stop MARL Virginia/Stop Valley Link Virginia and the Piedmont Environmental Council are actively organizing. Fluvanna County residents pushed back at a March 2026 community meeting. A Virginia delegate introduced a bill to limit where high-voltage transmission lines can be built.

The Danville-Pittsylvania RIFA voted unanimously on March 9, 2026 to approve SAC III Acquisition Co. (affiliated with Stack Infrastructure) to purchase 2,990 of 3,528 acres at the Berry Hill Megasite for $238,000/acre ($737.8M total). The draft performance agreement projects $73 billion investment and 2,050 jobs at $80,500 avg salary over 30 years. Phase 1 deadline: June 2027 (450 jobs, $6.4B investment in first 7 years). Minimum power: 299 MW by end of 2028 from Appalachian Power. The Danville-Pittsylvania economic development group is now urging the state to keep the data center tax incentive, arguing it is essential for rural Virginia economic transformation. RIFA Chairman Vic Ingram called the deal 'life-changing.'

The Digital Gateway lawsuit reached the Virginia Court of Appeals. Oral arguments were held on February 25, 2026 before a three-judge panel (Judges Raphael, Beales, and Bernhard) in Arlington. The case centers on whether Prince William County properly notified the public about the 2,100-acre rezoning for 37 data centers near Manassas National Battlefield Park. The Court of Appeals had previously stayed Judge Irving's August 2025 ruling that voided the rezonings. PWC supervisors voted to spend an additional $400K to defend the Digital Gateway, on top of prior legal costs. A written decision is pending. If the original ruling stands, it would void the largest proposed data center corridor in the world (22M+ sq ft).

Prince William County Board of Supervisors voted 4-3 on March 3, 2026 to approve the Hornbaker Road data center (571,000 sq ft on 40 acres along Prince William Parkway), located outside the Data Center Opportunity Zone Overlay District. Chair Deshundra Jefferson, Coles Supervisor Yesli Vega, and Gainesville Supervisor George Stewart dissented. In the same meeting, the board voted 6-0 (Jefferson abstaining) to initiate a Zoning Text Amendment that would eliminate by-right approval for data centers within the overlay district and require special use permits for all future projects countywide. The Coalition to Protect Prince William County actively opposed the Hornbaker project.
opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County

updates.summary: Hanover County Planning Commission voted unanimously on February 19, 2026 to defer its vote on Tract's proposal to rezone 430 acres at 13074 Mountain Road and 12517 Winns Church Road for the Mountain Road Technology Park. The 900 MW project would feature five building areas on 131 acres (31% of site), consuming an average 600,000 gallons/day of water (peak 2M gallons/day). A balloon test was conducted to visualize building heights. Community meeting on February 5 drew mostly opponents raising concerns about energy demand, infrastructure, traffic, and environmental impacts. The commission will reconsider at its March 19, 2026 meeting.

The Wise County 'Maverick Project' led by Wise Innovation Hub LLC (co-founded by Ross Litkenhous of Oasis Digital Properties) would build nine data center buildings (~240,000 sq ft each, ~2M sq ft total) at Lonesome Pine Regional Business and Technology Park. Red Post Energy (Houston-based, CEO Lance Medlin) signed a letter of intent for on-site gas power generation starting at 100 MW, scaling to 500-600 MW over 10 years. Would return 20-30% of electricity to the grid. Construction jobs: 500-700; power plant jobs: 140-260; DC operations: 30-50/building. Projected tax revenue: $3-5M/building. Wise County's low equipment tax rate (24 cents/$100 vs Loudoun's $4.15) is a draw. Appalachian Voices opposes the fossil fuel infrastructure. No rezoning required (already industrial). County industrial development supervisor Brian Falin emphasized community involvement.

The 2026 General Assembly session ended March 14 with no budget, punting to a special session on April 23, 2026 called by Gov. Spanberger. The $1.6 billion/year data center sales tax exemption remains the central dispute: Senate Appropriations Chair Louise Lucas demands ending it by Jan 1, 2027; House Appropriations Chair Luke Torian wants to preserve existing contracts. Spanberger favors caution. The exemption covers sales tax on equipment for data centers investing $150M+ and creating 50+ jobs (or $70M/10 jobs in economically distressed areas). Currently set to expire 2035. Danville-Pittsylvania and rural lawmakers argue ending it would kill rural economic development prospects. IBEW lobbied to keep the incentive for construction jobs.

HB 897, which would have required data centers to restrict CO2 emissions and move toward clean energy to qualify for the sales tax exemption, passed the House but died in the Senate during the 2026 session. The bill represented the most ambitious attempt to tie environmental conditions to Virginia's data center tax breaks.

The DEQ diesel generator issue advanced legislatively: HB 507 (Tier IV generator mandate) passed both chambers and awaits the Governor's signature. Additionally, HB 1502 (Del. Guzman's one-year DEQ study on standby generators) and SB 336 (Sen. Roem's SCC study on cleaner generators) were both continued to 2027. The VCU study finding that data center diesel generators in Northern Virginia emit more pollutants than natural gas plants has been repeatedly cited in legislative debate.

legislative_session_summary.note: Comprehensive summary of all VA data center bills from the 2026 General Assembly session

bills_passed_awaiting_governor.description: Mandates DEQ deny air permits for data center generators after July 2026 unless Tier IV or equivalent
Directs Department of Energy to study utility demand-response programs; report due November 1
Commission Department of Energy study on utilizing data center waste heat; report due September 1
Extends Dominion Energy and Appalachian Power weatherization programs for low-income households

bills_in_conference_committee.sponsors: Sen. Danica Roem / Del. Josh Thomas (D-Prince William)
bills_in_conference_committee.description: Site assessment for 100+ MW facilities: impacts on agriculture, parks, historic sites, forestland, noise within 500 ft. Senate version requires industrial zoning; House does not.

bills_in_conference_committee.sponsors: Sen. Kannan Srinivasan (D-Loudoun) / Del. Liz Guzman (D-Prince William)
bills_in_conference_committee.description: Water usage transparency: Senate version requires aggregate reporting to State Water Control Board; House version for local rezoning/permit requests

bills_that_died_or_continued.description: Clean energy/CO2 restrictions tied to tax exemption eligibility
SCC authority to review environmental/reliability impacts of high-load users
One-year data center moratorium until July 2028 or all interconnection requests fulfilled
One-year DEQ study on standby generators and pollution mitigation
SCC study on requiring cleaner-burning generators
Public hearing requirement for land transactions involving 69+ kV power facilities
Energy information sharing among data centers and agencies

budget_special_session.governor_position: Caution about altering a policy credited with making VA the world's largest data center hub
budget_special_session.exemption_thresholds: $150M investment + 50 jobs (or $70M + 10 jobs in distressed areas)
